abstract: This paper investigates how the electoral performance of far-right parties in the 2024 European Parliament elections affected European voters' concerns about disinformation. Drawing on evidence from across EU member states, the authors examine whether stronger far-right showings are associated with heightened public worry about disinformation. The study contributes to understanding the political and contextual drivers of citizen perceptions of information integrity in contemporary European democracies.
key_claims: Far-right party performance in the 2024 European Parliament elections shapes the level of disinformation concern among European voters.
Electoral context, and not only individual-level predispositions, influences how citizens perceive the threat of disinformation.
methods: Empirical analysis using data from the 2024 European Parliament elections context.
Cross-national comparison across EU member states linking far-right electoral outcomes to voter concerns about disinformation.
findings: Variation in far-right electoral performance across countries is associated with variation in voters' disinformation concerns (specific direction and magnitude not available from the abstract alone).
contributions: Connects research on far-right party success with research on public perceptions of disinformation in the European context.
Provides empirical evidence from the 2024 EP elections, a recent and politically salient case, on contextual drivers of disinformation concern.
Advances understanding of how electoral outcomes can shape citizens' attitudes about information ecosystems in the EU.
framing: The paper sits at the intersection of European elections research, far-right party studies, and the political communication literature on disinformation. It positions itself within debates about how populist and far-right politics interact with citizens' trust in and concerns about the integrity of public information.
